Slight opposition due to low openness to new systems and authoritarian preference for stable majorities, though populist anti-elitism provides some counterweight.
Moderate support from high GAL-TAN pluralism (69.0) and low trust in current system, but tempered by low political efficacy and a preference for stability from high system justification.

Moderate openness to experience (55.91) and a preference for participatory democracy (53.91) provide mild support, but high system justification (81.78) and low populist attitudes (3.92) limit enthusiasm.

High participatory democracy preference (68.91) and strong populist people-centrism (76.39) favor proportional representation, but very high traditional-authoritarian nationalism (95.53) prefers stable majorities, yielding moderate support.
High openness and low authoritarianism favor proportional representation, but strong national identity and preference for stability temper support.
High participatory democracy preference (95.8) and openness favor proportional representation, but strong national identification and system justification create caution.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in judiciary, very low authoritarian submission, and moderate external efficacy support protecting judicial independence, though past incarceration experience and low agreeableness-trust add caution.

Very high system justification (96.05) and political system justification (93.16), combined with high conscientiousness and strong rule-of-law orientation, produce clear support for judicial independence, despite low personal trust in the judiciary.
Mild support from general system justification and belief in rule of law, but tempered by very low trust in judiciary and high anti-elitism.
Moderate support from high system justification (92.88) and low deference to authority (7.99), wanting courts to be independent from political branches despite personal distrust.
High system justification (81.78) and respect for authority (68.29) support judicial independence, though low trust in judiciary (36.75) and personal justice system contact create some ambivalence.

High trust in judiciary (66.63) and strong authority-subversion morality (89.38) support judicial independence, while high populist anti-elitism (84.63) and disruptor tendencies (99.62) question elite judicial power, resulting in clear but tempered support.
High trust in judiciary and low authority subversion support independence, while high populist anti-elitism adds skepticism of judicial power.
Very high trust in judiciary (83.86) and system justification (90.28) strongly support judicial independence, reinforced by high agreeableness.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate general institutional trust and high external efficacy are offset by low trust in straightforwardness, moderate populist anti-elitism, and lived bureaucratic friction as a refugee.

Very low trust in government (1.2) and low external political efficacy (33.84) create mild distrust, but high conscientiousness (94.39) and personal experience in the public sector temper this with some respect for bureaucratic competence.
Clear distrust driven by extreme anti-elitism, low trust in government, and low agreeableness, though slightly moderated by authoritarian submission to bureaucratic authority.
Slight distrust from low external efficacy (18.45) and moderate institutional trust scores, but offset by high bonding social capital and personal experience with credential recognition.
Low trust in government (40.08) and low agreeableness (36.89) reduce trust, but high system justification (81.78) and moderate political efficacy (70.61) prevent strong distrust.

High system justification (political system 69.56) and very high agreeableness trust (91.31) support trust, but high populist anti-elitism (84.63) and low external political efficacy (45.82) create skepticism, yielding near-neutral mild support.
Moderate trust due to high political trust but low agreeableness and high anti-elitism create suspicion of bureaucratic elites.
Moderate institutional trust and high system justification provide baseline trust, but populist anti-elitism and low external efficacy reduce confidence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in electoral process (profile) is nearly canceled by very high anti-elitism, suspicion of institutions (low agreeableness-trust), and moderate ethnic nationalism, leaving near-neutrality.

Very low trust in institutions (4.16) and low external political efficacy (33.84) create mild distrust, but very high system justification (96.05) and strong national identification (87.0) pull toward neutrality, resulting in near-ambivalent trust.
Mild distrust from low institutional trust and high anti-elitism, though conventionalism and authoritarian submission provide some baseline confidence in electoral procedures.
Mild distrust from low trust in government (33.11), low external efficacy (18.45), and personal experience of discrimination, but tempered by high system justification (92.88).
High voting participation (98.71) and system justification (81.78) support trust, but low political trust in parliament (40.46) and personal justice system contact create mild, not strong, trust.

High trust in electoral process (implied by high political system justification 69.56) and very high agreeableness support trust, but high populist Manichean worldview (97.65) and collective narcissism (82.61) create suspicion of elite manipulation, yielding mild support.
High political trust and system justification support trust, but high populist anti-elitism and low agreeableness create some doubt.
Trust in electoral process is moderate; high system justification and political trust support it, while populist manichean worldview and anti-elitism create doubts.
